data-cleanup-task.label: Data Cleanup Task
data-cleanup-task.summary: Use string functions and filtering to clean up data.

sketch2.title: Filtering Out Test and Draft Records

intro-data-cleanup.title: Why Data Cleanup Matters
intro-data-cleanup.bodyMarkdown: In real-world databases, data often needs cleaning before it can be used for analysis. For example, test items, draft products, or inconsistent text entries can clutter results. SQL provides tools to filter, standardize, and clean up data using functions and conditions. In this topic, you'll practice filtering out test records, updating text fields, and using string functions to prepare data for reporting.

string-functions-overview.title: String Functions for Cleanup
string-functions-overview.bodyMarkdown: SQL string functions like `UPPER()`, `LOWER()`, and `TRIM()` help standardize text data. For example, you can use `UPPER(name)` to convert all product names to uppercase, or `TRIM(notes)` to remove extra spaces from notes. These functions are useful for making data consistent and easier to analyze.

filtering-records.title: Filtering Out Test and Draft Records
filtering-records.bodyMarkdown: Often, you need to exclude test or draft records from your results. In the `inventory_items` table, the `is_test` column marks test items (1 for test, 0 for real), and the `status` column indicates if an item is 'active', 'draft', or 'inactive'. Use `WHERE` clauses to filter out unwanted records, such as `WHERE is_test = 0 AND status = 'active'`.

single-choice-filter-test-items.title: Filtering Out Test Items
single-choice-filter-test-items.prompt: Which SQL condition will exclude all test items from the `inventory_items` table?
single-choice-filter-test-items.hint: Look for the value that means 'not a test'.

help.concept: The `is_test` column uses 1 for test items and 0 for real items.
help.hint_1: You want only real items, not test ones.

multi-choice-cleanup-steps.title: Common Data Cleanup Steps
multi-choice-cleanup-steps.prompt: Which of the following are common steps in cleaning up data in SQL? (Select all that apply.)
multi-choice-cleanup-steps.hint: Think about actions that make data more consistent and useful.

help.concept: Data cleanup involves removing unwanted data and making text consistent.
help.hint_1: Standardizing text and removing unnecessary records are part of cleanup.
help.hint_2: Trimming whitespace and converting case are also common cleanup tasks.

options.b: Converting text to a standard case

drag-reorder-cleanup-process.title: Order the Data Cleanup Steps
drag-reorder-cleanup-process.prompt: Arrange these data cleanup steps in the most logical order.
drag-reorder-cleanup-process.hint: Start by removing unwanted records before standardizing text.

help.concept: A logical cleanup process filters first, then standardizes text, and finally checks for missing data.
help.hint_2: After filtering, clean up text, then check for missing values.

tokens.t1: Filter out test and draft items
tokens.t4: Check for missing or null values

fill-blank-choice-string-function.title: Choosing the Right String Function
fill-blank-choice-string-function.prompt: Which SQL function would you use to remove extra spaces from the start and end of a text field?
fill-blank-choice-string-function.hint: This function cleans up spaces at the edges of text.

help.concept: The `the missing term()` function removes leading and trailing spaces from text.
help.hint_1: It's not about changing case or counting characters.
help.hint_2: It's the function that removes whitespace from both ends.

code-input-cleanup-query.title: Write a Cleanup Query
code-input-cleanup-query.prompt: Write a SQL query to select all columns from `inventory_items`, but only for items that are not test items (`is_test = 0`) and have status 'active'.
code-input-cleanup-query.hint: Use a WHERE clause with both conditions.

help.concept: Combine multiple conditions in the WHERE clause using AND.
help.hint_1: Filter for rows where `is_test` is 0 and `status` is 'active'.
help.hint_2: Use both conditions together in your WHERE clause.
